title: PHPUnit's error handler does not respect `@` error suppression and forwards suppressed warnings to previous error handler
author: calebdw
body:
## Summary

PHPUnit 13's `ErrorHandler::__invoke()` intercepts warnings from `@`-suppressed calls (e.g., `@include`) and forwards them via `forwardToPreviousErrorHandler()` without checking whether `error_reporting()` excludes the error level. This breaks the `@` suppression contract.

## Steps to reproduce

1. Register a custom error handler (or use a framework like Laravel that registers one via `HandleExceptions::handleError()`)
2. Run tests under PHPUnit 13
3. Have code that uses `@include` on a non-existent file (or any `@`-suppressed call that triggers a warning)

## Expected behavior

The `@` operator sets `error_reporting()` to exclude the suppressed level. PHPUnit's error handler should check this and return early, as the PHP manual documents:

> If you have set a custom error handler function with `set_error_handler()` then it will still get called, but this custom error handler can (and should) call `error_reporting()` which will return `0` when the call that triggered the error was prepended by an `@`.

## Actual behavior

PHPUnit's error handler intercepts the warning and forwards it to the previous error handler (e.g., Laravel's `HandleExceptions::handleError()`), which then throws a fatal error.

Error chain:
```
@include('nonexistent.php')
  -> PHPUnit ErrorHandler->__invoke()
    -> forwardToPreviousErrorHandler()
      -> Laravel HandleExceptions->handleError()
        -> throws ErrorException
```

## Suggested fix

Check `error_reporting()` before forwarding:

```php
if (!(error_reporting() & $severity)) {
    return true;
}
```

## Context

- PHPUnit version: 13.x
- PHP version: 8.2+
- Discovered via Larastan CI: https://github.com/larastan/larastan/actions/runs/29059934565/job/86259357412
- Related PHPStan PR (rejected, maintainers indicated this is a userland error handler issue): https://github.com/phpstan/phpstan-src/pull/6034

title: Warn when `failOnAllIssues="true"` is combined with an explicitly disabled fine-grained `failOn*` setting
author: sebastianbergmann
body:
`failOnAllIssues="true"` is a blanket setting that enables failing on all issue types. It is not meant to be overridden by more fine-grained `failOn*` settings; the `--do-not-fail-on-*` CLI options are the intended mechanism for carve-outs. This works as designed (see #6829).

However, when `failOnAllIssues="true"` is configured together with an explicitly disabled fine-grained setting such as `failOnSelfDeprecation="false"`, the fine-grained setting is silently ignored. A test runner warning should be emitted for this combination so that the conflict is no longer silent.

#### Proposed behavior

Given this XML configuration:

```xml
<phpunit failOnAllIssues="true"
         failOnSelfDeprecation="false">
```

a test runner warning such as this should be emitted:

```
failOnSelfDeprecation="false" has no effect because failOnAllIssues is enabled; use the --do-not-fail-on-self-deprecation CLI option to not fail on self-deprecations
```

The warning should be emitted for each fine-grained `failOn*` attribute that is explicitly set to `false` in the XML configuration file while `failOnAllIssues` is enabled (whether via the XML configuration file or via the `--fail-on-all-issues` CLI option).

title: `failOn*="false"` XML attributes are silently ignored when `failOnAllIssues="true"` is set
author: Ziffrit

title: Issue is reported when custom error handler checks `error_reporting()` output dynamically
author: Slamdunk

title: Issue is reported even when previously registered error handler turns the error into an exception
author: Slamdunk
body:
| Q                   | A
| --------------------| ---------------
| PHPUnit version     | 13.2
| PHP version         | 8.5
| Installation Method | Composer

#### Summary

We set our custom error handler in the `bootstrap` because it's valid for all the application and this is how production behaves as well.
But apparently https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/issues/5873 changed error handler priorities, and since `13.2` PHPUnit's one gets in the way so we are not able to test ours properly anymore.

#### How to reproduce

`phpunit.xml`

```xml
<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<phpunit xmlns:xsi="http://www.w3.org/2001/XMLSchema-instance"
         xsi:noNamespaceSchemaLocation="vendor/phpunit/phpunit/phpunit.xsd"
         cacheResult="false"
         bootstrap="bootstrap.php"
         failOnAllIssues="true"
         displayDetailsOnAllIssues="true"
>
    <testsuites>
        <testsuite name="default">
            <directory>tests</directory>
        </testsuite>
    </testsuites>
</phpunit>
```

`bootstrap.php`

```php
<?php

final class MyErrorException extends \ErrorException {}

error_reporting(E_ALL);
set_error_handler(static function ($level, $message, $file = null, $line = null): bool {
    throw new MyErrorException($message, $level, $level, $file, $line);
});

require_once __DIR__ . '/src/Source.php';
```

`src/Source.php`

```php
<?php declare(strict_types=1);

final class Source
{
    public function triggerWarning(): void
    {
        // Using ZipArchive to raise a real E_WARNING
        $zip = new \ZipArchive();
        $zip->open(uniqid('/tmp/test_'), \ZipArchive::CREATE | \ZipArchive::EXCL | \ZipArchive::CHECKCONS);
        $zip->addFile(uniqid('/tmp/non_existent_file_'), 'foo');
    }
}
```

`tests/SourceTest.php`

```php
<?php declare(strict_types=1);

final class SourceTest extends \PHPUnit\Framework\TestCase
{
    public function testWarning(): void
    {
        $this->expectException(MyErrorException::class);

        (new Source)->triggerWarning();
    }
}
```

PHPUnit output as of version `13.2`

```
W                                                                   1 / 1 (100%)

Time: 00:00, Memory: 18.00 MB

1 test triggered 1 PHP warning:

1) /tmp/src/Source.php:19
ZipArchive::addFile(): No such file or directory

Triggered by:

* SourceTest::testWarning
  /tmp/tests/SourceTest.php:16

OK, but there were issues!
Tests: 1, Assertions: 1, Warnings: 1.
```

#### Expected behavior

PHPUnit output until `13.1.14`

```
.                                                                   1 / 1 (100%)

Time: 00:00, Memory: 18.00 MB

OK (1 test, 1 assertion)
```

title: Make dependency on `ext/filter` explicit
author: sebastianbergmann
body:
The ext-filter extension is only required to pass the `tests/end-to-end/extension-xml/phar-extension.phpt` test. The `vendor/phar-io/manifest/` dependency uses `filter_var()` without declaring an explicit dependency on `ext-filter`. It may make sense to also list `'filter'` in the `$requiredExtensions`.

_Originally posted by @TimWolla in https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/issues/6803#issuecomment-4864281686_

title: Code generated for test double of interface with constructor triggers deprecation on PHP 8.6
author: pcf0
body:
| Q                   | A
| --------------------| ---------------
| PHPUnit version     | 13.2.2
| PHP version         | 8.6.0-dev / 8.6.0alpha1
| Installation Method | Composer

#### Summary

PHP 8.6 will deprecate returning a value from constructor.
See: https://wiki.php.net/rfc/deprecate-return-value-from-construct

#### Current behavior

In my scenario, an interface contains the `__construct()` method to enforce its implementation.
I want to create a stub using `TestCase::createStub(...)`.
Because return types are not allowed here, PHPUnit determines the return type to be `\SebastianBergmann\Type\UnknownType`, which causes a return statement to be generated in the constructor.
This, in turn, triggers the following deprecation warning:
```
.../vendor/phpunit/phpunit/src/Framework/MockObject/Generator/DoubledClass.php(51) : eval()'d code:43
Returning a value from a constructor is deprecated
```

Relevant code:
https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/type/blob/main/src/ReflectionMapper.php#L76-L78
https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/blob/13.2.2/src/Framework/MockObject/Generator/DoubledMethod.php#L187-L193

#### How to reproduce
1. Install/compile PHP with at least Version 8.6.0alpha1
2. create a composer project:
```json
{
    "require-dev": {
        "phpunit/phpunit": "^13.2"
    },
    "require": {
        "php": "^8.5"
    }
}
```
3. create a `test.php` with following content:
```php
<?php
declare(strict_types=1);

interface MyInterface
{
    public function __construct();
}

class Test extends \PHPUnit\Framework\TestCase
{
    public function testSomething(): void {
        static::createStub(MyInterface::class);
        static::assertTrue(true);
    }
}
```

4. run command:
```bash
$ vendor/bin/phpunit test.php --display-all-issues
```
5. See output:
```
PHPUnit 13.2.2 by Sebastian Bergmann and contributors.

Runtime:       PHP 8.6.0-dev

D                                                                   1 / 1 (100%)

Time: 00:00, Memory: 16.00 MB

1 test triggered 1 PHP deprecation:

1) /home/gitlab-runner/test/vendor/phpunit/phpunit/src/Framework/MockObject/Generator/DoubledClass.php(51) : eval()'d code:43
Returning a value from a constructor is deprecated

Triggered by:

* Test::testSomething
  /home/gitlab-runner/test/test.php:11

OK, but there were issues!
Tests: 1, Assertions: 1, Deprecations: 1.
```

```bash
$ composer info | sort
myclabs/deep-copy            1.13.4 Create deep copies (clones) of your objects
nikic/php-parser             5.7.0  A PHP parser written in PHP
phar-io/manifest             2.0.4  Component for reading phar.io manifest information from a PHP Archive (PHAR)
phar-io/version              3.2.1  Library for handling version information and constraints
phpunit/php-code-coverage    14.2.2 Library that provides collection, processing, and rendering functionality for PHP code coverage information.
phpunit/php-file-iterator    7.0.0  FilterIterator implementation that filters files based on a list of suffixes.
phpunit/php-invoker          7.0.0  Invoke callables with a timeout
phpunit/php-text-template    6.0.0  Simple template engine.
phpunit/php-timer            9.0.0  Utility class for timing
phpunit/phpunit              13.2.2 The PHP Unit Testing framework.
sebastian/cli-parser         5.0.0  Library for parsing CLI options
sebastian/comparator         8.3.0  Provides the functionality to compare PHP values for equality
sebastian/complexity         6.0.0  Library for calculating the complexity of PHP code units
sebastian/diff               9.0.0  Diff implementation
sebastian/environment        9.3.2  Provides functionality to handle HHVM/PHP environments
sebastian/exporter           8.1.0  Provides the functionality to export PHP variables for visualization
sebastian/file-filter        1.0.0  Library for filtering files
sebastian/git-state          1.0.0  Library for describing the state of a Git checkout
sebastian/global-state       9.0.1  Snapshotting of global state
sebastian/lines-of-code      5.0.1  Library for counting the lines of code in PHP source code
sebastian/object-enumerator  8.0.0  Traverses array structures and object graphs to enumerate all referenced objects
sebastian/object-reflector   6.0.0  Allows reflection of object attributes, including inherited and non-public ones
sebastian/recursion-context  8.0.0  Provides functionality to recursively process PHP variables
sebastian/type               7.0.1  Collection of value objects that represent the types of the PHP type system
sebastian/version            7.0.0  Library that helps with managing the version number of Git-hosted PHP projects
staabm/side-effects-detector 1.0.5  A static analysis tool to detect side effects in PHP code
theseer/tokenizer            2.0.1  A small library for converting tokenized PHP source code into XML and potentially other formats
```

#### Expected behavior

No deprecation message.

title: Deprecation triggered outside of tests cannot be ignored
author: nikophil
body:
| Q                   | A
| --------------------| ---------------
| PHPUnit version     | 13.2
| PHP version         | any
| Installation Method | Composer

#### Summary

Hi,

this one is a little bit edgy, but still it seems like a bug. If some deprecations are triggered during the "requirements" phase it cannot be ignored, and some code which did not fail in 13.1 now fails in 13.2

#### How to reproduce

Given the following code:

```php
// SomeTest.php

use Some\Vendor\DeprecatedClass;
use PHPUnit\Framework\Attributes\RequiresMethod;
use PHPUnit\Framework\Attributes\Test;
use PHPUnit\Framework\TestCase;

class SomeTest extends TestCase
{
    #[Test]
    #[RequiresMethod(DeprecatedClass::class, 'foo')]
    public function testSomething(): void
    {
        self::assertTrue(true);
    }
}

// DeprecatedClass.php

trigger_deprecation('1.0', 'some/vendor', 'deprec!');

class DeprecatedClass
{
}
```

Running the test emits a deprecation which cannot be ignored:

```
➜ vendor/bin/phpunit
PHPUnit 13.2.1 by Sebastian Bergmann and contributors.

Runtime:       PHP 8.4.22
Configuration: /path/to/phpunit.xml

S                                                                   1 / 1 (100%)

Time: 00:00.001, Memory: 18.00 MB

There was 1 deprecation triggered outside of tests:

1) /path/to/vendor/symfony/deprecation-contracts/function.php:25
Since 1.0 some/vendor: deprec!

OK, but there were issues!
Tests: 1, Assertions: 0, Deprecations: 1, Skipped: 1.
```

`--generate-baseline baseline.xml` generates an empty file. `#[IgnoreDeprecation]` has no effect

and then if the test suite is run with `failOnDeprecation="true"`, the test suite exits with 1

thank you for your help

title: Use SIGQUIT instead SIGINT to handle interrupts
author: esler
body:
Hello,

PHPUnit introduced interrupt handling in #4201, which is great. Many years ago, I wrote a small extension to do exactly that: [esler/phpunit-graceful-interrupt](https://packagist.org/packages/esler/phpunit-graceful-interrupt).

However, in my library I chose to intercept `SIGQUIT` (`Ctrl+\`) instead of `SIGINT` (`Ctrl+C`). The [reasoning](https://github.com/esler/phpunit-graceful-interrupt/wiki) was that sometimes you need to interrupt a stuck test immediately. So I reserved `Ctrl+C` for a hard stop and used a different signal for a graceful one.

On the other hand, not many users are familiar with the `Ctrl+\` shortcut. `Ctrl+C` is the more natural choice for most people, which creates a tension between discoverability and flexibility. 🤔

I'd be happy to write a corresponding PR if you consider this a worthwhile change.

title: Allow negative priorities for hook methods
author: nikophil
body:
| Q                   | A
| --------------------| ---------------
| PHPUnit version     | 13.2.1
| PHP version         | any
| Installation Method | Composer

Hi!

It seems [this PR](https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/pull/6120) has been rollback by [this commit](https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/commit/82848be7178feec5a95ca16e3f7c8375b8da09a1) merged in 13.2.1

But negative priorities are perfectly valid, they allow to chose the given hook to occur after a hook method which does not have a priority 

would you be OK to let those priorities be negative again?

title: The composer audit is called twice in the ci pipeline
author: bebehr
body:
**Version**
main-Branch: https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/blob/main/.github/workflows/ci.yaml

Across different versions, not quite sure but I guess for all version of phpunit from 9.6 to 13.2

**Problem**
https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/blob/ea25442a475d1b01e19a5389d545306ca96b2092/.github/workflows/ci.yaml#L90-L94

**Analysis**
There were two commits which do the same:
- https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/commit/d70691052b08722c52f7dc573726a4cfb575506b
- https://github.com/sebastianbergmann/phpunit/commit/6eda9e8ebfed4799a80229476654bbc665717911

This ends up in calling composer audit twice unnecessarily.

**Proposed solution**
Remove duplicate call and save a few resources in the CI pipeline.

title: Retried tests do not report total test duration, only the last attempt’s one
author: mondrake

title: Use of `#[Retry]` and `#[Repeat]` breaks `#[Group]` metadata
author: mondrake

title: Native parallel test execution
author: sebastianbergmann
body:
## Disclaimer

The purpose of this issue is to provide a space for exploring and discussing the architecture and design specifications of native parallel test execution implemented directly within PHPUnit. I would like to make it clear that I am not working on this because I think that existing solutions, such as Paratest and ParaUnit, are inadequate. I also want to make it clear that the existence of this issue does not mean that PHPUnit will support parallel test execution out of the box. For now, I am treating this as a thought experiment: what if parallel test execution were to be implemented natively in PHPUnit? Would that benefit end users? Would it be easier to maintain?

## Status Quo

At present, PHPUnit can only execute a test suite sequentially: `TextUI\TestRunner::run()` flattens the suite and calls `$test->run()` on each test in turn (`Framework\TestSuite::run()`). On multi-core machines this leaves most of the hardware idle. Third-party tools (paratest, paraunit) parallelize by running PHPUnit itself as a black-box subprocess per test file and then parsing its log output to reconstruct results. That approach is inherently lossy and cannot reproduce PHPUnit's own reporting fidelity.

## High-level implementation approach

PHPUnit already owns a high-fidelity mechanism for running a test in another process and reconstituting its outcome: the implementation of its process isolation feature. Native parallelism is therefore not a new subsystem bolted on top; it is the generalization of that implementation from "one synchronous child per test" to "N concurrent, reusable workers":

| Concern | Class | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Serialize a test into a runnable child script | `Framework\TestRunner\SeparateProcessTestRunner` | Templates one `TestCase` + config + (optional) global state into PHP code |
| Spawn a child and collect its output | `Util\PHP\JobRunner` | `proc_open`, feed code, collect stdout/stderr |
| Reconstitute a child's outcome in the parent | `Framework\TestRunner\ChildProcessResultProcessor` | Forwards events, imports `PassedTests`, sets result/status/assertions, merges coverage |
| Transport | nonce-prefixed serialized `stdClass` written to a temp file | `{events, passedTests, testResult, status, numAssertions, output, codeCoverage}` |
| Replay child events in the parent | `Event\Facade::forward(EventCollection)` | Re-dispatches a collected event stream through the parent dispatcher |
| Collect events in a child | `Event\Facade::initForIsolation()` → `CollectingDispatcher` | Accumulates events; `flush()` returns + resets them |

Every printer, logger, and report is a downstream subscriber of events. A child runs with a `CollectingDispatcher`, collects its events into an `EventCollection`, ships them home, and the parent replays them with `forward()`. Consequently, if a worker can ship its event stream to the parent, the parent's entire output/logging/result subsystem works unchanged:

```
TextUI\Application
        │  (selects runner when parallel workers > 1)
        ▼
TextUI\TestRunner ───────────────► TextUI\ParallelTestRunner
   (sequential, today)                      │
                                            ▼
                              Runner\Parallel\WorkerPool
                               │        │         │
                        Worker 1     Worker 2   Worker N   (PersistentWorker)
                               │        │         │
                          ┌────┴────────┴─────────┴─────┐
                          │  Scheduler / WorkQueue      │
                          └─────────────────────────────┘
                                       │  per-unit result envelopes
                                       ▼
                          Runner\Parallel\ResultAggregator
                          → Event\Facade::forward(...) in suite order
                          → PassedTests::import(), CodeCoverage::merge()
```

The parent process remains the single source of truth for output: it owns the `OutputFacade`, `TestResultFacade`, `CodeCoverage`, `ResultCache`, and the sealed event dispatcher. Workers never produce user-facing output; they only emit events that the parent replays.

`JobRunner::runProcess()` was synchronous: spawn one process, block in `stream_get_contents()`, `proc_close()`. A pool needs to keep N processes busy simultaneously.

Chosen mechanism: `proc_open()` and `stream_select()` over the workers' pipes.

Rationale:
- No new dependency or runtime requirement, it runs everywhere `proc_open()` already runs (the existing isolation requirement)
- Rejected alternatives: `ext-parallel` (requires ZTS; narrow availability) and `ext-pcntl` fork (no Windows; fragile fork-sharing of an initialized PHPUnit)

Granularity should be configurable, defaulting to per test class:

- per-suite: coarsest; good for several independent `<testsuite>`s
- per-class (default): preserves `#[BeforeClass]` / `#[AfterClass]` and intra-class ordering; matches paratest's default; bounds per-process overhead
- per-method: finest balancing, but breaks shared class fixtures (and likely has increased per-test coverage serialization cost, for example)

Distribution is a dynamic work-stealing queue, not static pre-chunking: each idle worker pulls the next unit, which self-balances against stragglers. The `ResultCache` (per-test durations, `Runner\ResultCache\`) can seed a longest-processing-time-first ordering so large units start early.

## Step 1:  Async-capable `JobRunner`

This has already been implemented in #6753.

## Step 2: Persistent worker

This has already been implemented in #6753.

## Step 3: Worker Pool and Scheduler

- `Runner\Parallel\WorkerPool` owns N `PersistentWorker`s and pumps their pipes with `stream_select()`
- Dynamic work-stealing queue; default per-class granularity; ordering seeded from `ResultCache`
- `TextUI\ParallelTestRunner` (or a branch in the existing runner) selects the parallel path when configured worker count > 1

## Step 4: ResultAggregator with ordered replay

Workers finish out of order; events carry telemetry and the printers assume suite-ordered arrival. The aggregator buffers each unit's `EventCollection` and forwards it to the parent dispatcher in deterministic suite order, releasing a unit only once all prior units have flushed. Output then matches sequential mode byte-for-byte (good for CI diffs), at the cost of latency and memory.

## Step 5: Correctness and robustness

- **Dependencies:** `#[Depends]` / `ExecutionOrderDependency`: dependent tests must land in the same worker in order (the `TestSuiteSorter` graph already computes this)
- **Stop conditions:** `--stop-on-*` / interruption need a broadcast halt; in-flight units are abandoned, so the stop point is no longer exactly
  deterministic (maybe "only" a documentation issue)
- **Suite hooks:** before-first-test / after-last-test hook methods run once in the parent (or a designated worker), not per worker
- **Resource partitioning:** expose a worker-identity environment variable so test fixtures can choose a per-worker database/port/temporary directory/...
- **Crash/retry:** detect a dead pipe / abnormal exit, attribute it to the in-flight unit, optionally retry once on a fresh worker, keep other workers running

title: Testrunner-Option to skip the first N tests
author: ThomasMeschke
body:
Assume I have a test suite consisting of 3.000 tests. 
Assume I am running them with the --stop-on-defect option. 
Assume an error or failure is found after 2.500 tests. 
Assume this error would make some of the next 500 tests fail as well, 
due to being follow-up implications or simply affected by the same issue. 

Currently, I have to re-run all the tests just to maybe find the next issue e.g. in test number 2.513, and so on.

I would love to have an option to stop on the first defect, fix the found defect, and then sort of continue the test execution from where I left of, in the sense of skipping the first N tests (here: 2.499) and start the execution with test number 2.500 (to proof the fix) onward, without having to re-run the first 2.499 tests of which I assume they work. 

This could speed up the "am I done yet?" phase of a refactoring an would leave the "run all and everything" moment until the very end.

title: Environment-variable attributes reject empty-string values since PHPUnit 13.2.0
author: cs278

title: Test is not run when `--filter` matches the name of a data set but not the name of the test method
author: lpartington
body:
| Q                   | A
| --------------------| ---------------
| PHPUnit version     | 13.2.0
| PHP version         | 8.5.7
| Installation Method | Composer

#### Summary
When specifying a --filter string containing a key, or part of a key, for DataProvider test cases, the matching test cases should be executed. However, for phpunit 13.2.0+ none of the cases are executed.

#### Current behavior
The following snippet uses a DataProvider to add several test cases named 'case1', 'case2', and so on. 
`phpunit --filter case1` should run the case1 test, but no tests are executed (the output is "No tests executed!".

```
<?php
namespace Tests;
use PHPUnit\Framework\TestCase;
use PHPUnit\Framework\Attributes\{Test, DataProvider};

class DataProviderTest extends TestCase
{
    public static function data()
    {
        $cases = [];
        for ($i = 1; $i <= 4; ++$i)
            $cases["case{$i}"] = [$i];
        return $cases;
    }

    #[Test]
    #[DataProvider('data')]
    public function test($case_id)
    {
        $this->assertTrue(true);
    }
}
```

#### How to reproduce
Install phpunit 13.2.0, 13.2.x-dev or 13.3.x-dev.
Using the above code snippet, run `phpunit --filter case1`

#### Expected behavior
The test case with key = "case1" should be executed.
Note that case1 is executed when using phpunit 13.1.14.

#### Supporting Info

sebastian/complexity         6.0.0            Library for calculating the complexity of PHP code units
sebastian/diff               9.0.0            Diff implementation
sebastian/environment        9.3.2            Provides functionality to handle HHVM/PHP environments

title: Introduce a bootstrap method for DeprecationFilter
author: mondrake
body:
WRT to the recently introduced `DeprecationFilter` #6710, I was wondering whether it would make sense to introduce a `bootstrap()` method (similarly to other PHPUnit extensions) or something like that, so that preparing data to be used by the `ignore()` method can be made available to it in a structured manner.

Drupal would have to preload the patterns to be ignored from a file.

Obviously this can happen in the constructor of the `DeprecationFilter` implementation, but I'd be wary to deal with I/O during object construction; a dedicated method would make DX more effective I think.

Thanks
